During Japanese occupancy, many Chinese children did not have opportunities to attend schools, and this situation happened until some years after the War ended. Venerable Cihang and the head of compatriots living in Penang founded an elementary school under the Buddhist college at Penang as which had been established before. Venerable Cihang immigrated to Singapore on 1943, and then he founded monthly magazine entitled ’Humanistic Buddhism.’ In the Fall of 1948, he came to Taiwan and was in charge of Taiwanese Buddhist college. He passed away at Mile neiyuan at Xizhi, Taiwan.

Title: Prajñā of Words, Artistic Creation and the Practice of Buddhism: the Example of Master Xinyun’s Verses Uttered by Xingyun
Abstract: This article takes Master Xingyun’s Verses Uttered by Xingyun as the main working material. It argues two points: one, Prajnā in writing could lead to meditation state; two, artistic creation and the practice of Buddhism. Both issues centre on the idea that meditation states are not to be expressed in word or writing; Meditation once being said or written becomes dross, or intellectual understanding. But it must be said, thus there is the approach of understanding meditation states through words. If meditation can be spoken, it must be something else other than meditation. Thus Master Xingyun describes meditation states with many analogies. In present paper I will discuss the Master’s ideas of meditation and practicing meditation, and what he promotes in Buddhist literary works, Buddhist artistic culture as a means to spread Buddhism in general and Humanistic Buddhism in particular.

Title: Korean Academics’ Interpretation and Understanding of the Taiwanese Humanistic Buddhism
Abstract: I have written on the comparison of Buddhism between Korea and Taiwan and put forward these points: in the last hundred years, Buddhism in both regions had gone through a similar path, i.e. being colonized by Japan for several decades, suffered from military persecution and anti-communist and pro-American polices, and historical turbulence like sangha tragedy, but they are now different in a huge way. Over the last several decades, after reflections on the traditional forest Buddhism, Humanistic Buddhism have been the mainstream Buddhism in Taiwan, it shows equality in gender, in clerics and the laity, and serving society. In the meantime, they promote Buddhism through education, especially producing more scholars of Buddhism. On the methods of propagating Buddhism, they have an all-round and multi-formed approach, making Buddhism enter people’s life in modern, internationalized and multiple ways. This all enhances the soft-power of Taiwanese Buddhism. In recent ten years or so, Korean Buddhists started to take notice of the flourishing of Taiwanese Humanistic Buddhism and ponder how Taiwanese Buddhism became the exemplary model of Buddhist development in Asia in such a short period. It is interesting that in 2011 learning and studying Taiwanese Humanistic Buddhism become popular among Korean Buddhists, we can even say that 2011 was the year for Korean Buddhists to follow Taiwanese Buddhism.

Title: Humanistic Buddhism in Hong Kong During the Early Chinese Republican period-A Case Study of Tung Lin Kok Yuen
Abstract: The early twentieth century was a time of unrest for China, marked by an unstable government and economy and an impending collapse of traditional culture and moral values. Buddhism, with no exception, was to undergo a transformation. Master Tai Xu, realizing the importance of Buddhist education that should be reformedin order to accord with modern society, raised the issue of constructing Humanistic Buddhism. In the Hong Kong and Macau areas, a devout Buddhist laywoman named Lady Clara Lin-Kok was very much praised by Master Tai Xu in the early history of the Republic of China’s Buddhist education system. She had far-reaching influence as a pioneer who took up the challenge to reform modern local Buddhist education. She established Tung Lin Kok Yuen as a free Buddhist school for children from poor families. After receiving education from Tung Lin Kok Yuen, some of those Buddhist nun students traveled overseas to the United States, Canada, or Southeast Asia to spread Buddhism. The school’s influence on Buddhist education in Hong Kong has continued well into the twenty-first century. From 1935 until now, Tung Lin Kok Yuen has had a deep impact on the development of Humanistic Buddhismin the world, and its impact continues to expand. How has an ordinary Buddhist laywoman had far-reaching views and a vow to promote Buddhism internationally in the early twentieth century? This paper will explore the literary historical documents from the early Republic of China and then further examine the communication with Buddhist monks and temples between Hong Kong and Southeast China.

Title: A Study of Master Thich Nhat Hanh’s Engaged Buddhism and Vietnamese Humanistic Buddhism
Abstract: Meditation Master Thich Nhat Hanh is currently the greatest Vietnamese Buddhist master. Starting from Vietnam he spread Humanistic Buddhism to France, America, Germany, Canada and other western countries, planting Buddhist philosophy in the daily life of people, then brought the teaching back to Vietnam which has exerted great and profound influence on contemporary Vietnamese Buddhism. This article first treats the Master’s idea of Engaged Buddhism in detail , through an analysis of the master ’s books of a huge number and the activities of his Plum Villeage the discussion will specially focus on how the master combines the thought of meditation and that of Pure Land and put them into the daily life of the mass. Then the article will discuss how the master ’s thoughts has been influencing people in Vietnam over the last twenty years despite the fact that there were confinements in political and religious policies and the local Sangha. Finally, the paper will deal with the effects of the Thich Nhat Nanh’s version of Humanistic Buddhism on all aspects of Vietnamese Buddhism regardless not being recognized publically due to political, religious reasons and turf mentality.

Title: The Latest Practice of Fo Guang Shan’s Humanistic Buddhism: Kaohsiung International Fruit Festival
Abstract: In more than 2000 years of the development in Buddhism history, Buddhism must comply with the special historical environments and the cultures of ethnic groups in different regions, thus giving rise to changes that are different from Indian Buddhism. From the History of the Chinese Buddhism Thoughts, Buddhism has not only integrated into the traditional Chinese culture, Buddhism also corresponds to the political and economical development of the present age, paving the way for teachings of Buddhism to be accepted and broadened by the contemporary era. Actively facing and resolving the barriers between Buddhism and the contemporary social trends, is the fundamental process in propagating the Buddhist doctrine. Such proactive characteristic to propagate the Dharma not only affects Buddhism, but it also affects the culture deeply. They present directly the localization, humanization, practical and popularization of Buddhism. For the purpose of implementing the fundamental concept of ’transformation of all beings’ and the key point to teaching all beings in Buddhism, Venerable Master Hsing Yun held the ’Kaohsiung Dashu International Fruit Festival’ since 2012. This is completely according to the needs of the present era. It allows the functions of the Monastery to explore beyond sutras recitation, worship to the Buddha and sightseeing. Humanistic Buddhism is one that places great emphasis to life. The purport of Humanistic Buddhism is ’the creation of happiness in family, the creation of equality in society, the creation of political democracy, the creation of inner mind Pureland’. Therefore, the scope of Humanistic Buddhism is inclusive of the scholars, farmers, artisans and merchants. However, reality is such that it is a challenge to reach out to scholars, artisans and merchants. The majority of farmers will have the residual force to pay greater attention to religious belief only when they make profits and can have happy and peaceful life. Organising the ’Kaohsiung Dashu International Fruit Festival’ is to practice Humanistic Buddhism. It also conforms to the basic theory of ’that is taught by the Buddha himself, that is needed by human beings, that is pure, and that is virtuous and beautiful’. Venerable Master Hsing Yun uses the modern methods, yet not losing the traditional spirit and theory of Buddhism. He has intentionally organised ’Kaohsiung Dashu International Fruit Festival’ to fulfill the implementation of Humanistic Buddhism. This article uses the implementation of Humanistic Buddhism Ideology through ’Kaohsiung Dashu International Frui t Festival’ to interpret and discuss ’why ’fruit festival’ can propagate Humanistic Buddhism?’. It links aptly to the study of the relationships of Venerable Master Hsing Yun and Lychee and the practices by Venerable Master Hsing Yun in ’Kaohsiung Dashu International Fruit Festival’. In the article, there are also discussions on how Venerable Master Hsing Yun breaks through the boundary between Buddhism participation in society and the traditional line, as well as to elaborate the idea of ’The Latest Practice of Fo Guang Shan’s Humanistic Buddhism’.

Title: A Study of ’Secular Spirit’ of Vietnamese Buddhism to the Humanistic Buddhism of Ven. Master Taixu
Abstract: Like the destiny of Vietnam, Vietnamese Buddhism has gone through days of hardships in the wake of the rise and fall of the country. Since Buddhism was initially introduced into Vietnam, it has become Vietnam’s leading ideology. Through integrating and developing with local culture continuously, the unique Vietnamese Buddhism was formed. Unlike Buddhism in Indian and Chinese Buddhism, Vietnamese Buddhism has spread to every corner of Vietnam and accompanied Vietnam through the most difficult period of nation-building war, and therefore has an extraordinary value. With the ’secular spirit’, Buddhism played an indispensable part in linking all walks of life in Vietnamese society, especially in Lý and Trần Dynasties (李朝、陳朝), Those were the exceptionally glorious era of Vietnamese Buddhism which received the greatest support from court and lay people. Buddhism had its the highest status in Vietnamese society. Various problems occurred within Buddhism and the decline of the significance of the qualification of monks resulted in the reformation of the sangha in late Trần Dynasty (1396). Regulations were set up to manage the sangha and numerous examinations were introduced to review the qualification of monks. Buddhism was revived in early 19th century. Under the leadership of Officer Master Xuanguang (炫光僧統), Chan Master Zhiguang (智光禪師) and Chan Master Thích Nhất Hạnh (一行禪師), Buddhism in Vietnam absorbed the idea of ’Humanistic Buddhism’ advocated by Venerable Master Taixu (太虛大師), and flourished in Vietnam. In 1960s, Ngô Đình Diệm (吳廷琰), the president of the Republic of Vietnam and a Catholic, whom launched pro-Catholic policies and persecuted Buddhism. People suffered a lot from his autocracy, corruption and oppressive policies. At that time, Vietnamese buddhist decided to come out to stand in with the public to resist the government. Numerous of monks and lay buddhist were killed by the government. The death by self-immolation of Thích Quảng Đức (釋廣德), a Vietnamese monk, in protest against the persecution of Buddhist brought the dictatorship of Ngô Đình Diệm to the end and brought a new era of the communist state of Vietnam and Humanistic Buddhism in Vietnam. Vietnamese Buddhism which has absorbed the idea of ’Humanistic Buddhism’ advocated by Venerable Master Taixu remains its uniqueness. Since Vietnamese Buddhism has a profound relationship with the country and its development, the ’secular spirit’ of Vietnamese Buddhism is fully and naturally integrated into the nation, without any emphasis or advocacy of any slogan. At present, development of ’secular spirit’ or ’Humanistic Buddhism’ in Vietnam reach a climax. Buddhism is neither the sole religion of the country, nor the leading ideology, but there is no denying that it still has its significance for Vietnam in terms of its meaning and its status.

Title: A Re-examination of Ven. Taixu’s太虛Jiànshè rénjiān jìngtŭ lùn建設人間淨土論(’On the Establishment of the Pure Land in the Human Realm’)
Abstract: Almost fifty years ago, Holmes Welch said that Ven. Taixu (Tàixū 太虛, 1890-1947) was ’widely regarded as the most important figure in the history of modern Chinese Buddhism.’ A tireless advocate for the modernization of Buddhism, he is credited with articulating a vision for a Buddhism adapted for modern society called ’Buddhism for Human Life’ (rénshēng fójiào人生佛教) or ’Buddhism for the Human Realm’ (rénjiān rójiào人間佛教, often rendered nowadays as ’Humanistic Buddhism’). Within this framework we often find the phrase ’The Pure Land in the Human Realm’ (rénjiān jìngtŭ人間淨土), generally understood not as the Buddha-land of Amitābha as a potential located within this present world. Under this rubric, Buddhists are counseled not to denigrate the world before them as defiled nor to place all of their hopes on rebirth in an idealized Pure Land after death, but to engage the present world in order to purify it and create a Pure Land here and now. Taixu’s major statement of this concept may be found in his essay ’On the Establishment of the Pure Land in the Human Realm’ (Jiànshè rénjiān jìngtŭ lùn建設人間淨土論). Don A. Pittman’s monograph on Taixu summarizes this essay in a way that brings Taixu’s reformist concerns to the forefront, and western scholars still take it as a statement of Taixu’s desire to direct modern Buddhism toward social reform. However, when one reads this essay, along with Taixu’s other texts on Pure Land, one finds it to be a very eclectic mix of the modern and the traditional in which aspiration for rebirth in Amitābha’s Pure Land is not at all discouraged. This presentation will bring back into discussion the full range of subjects addressed in this pivotal essay, and in the conclusion Taixu will appear as a transitional figure pointing toward the modernization of Chinese Buddhism rather than as a decisive modernist.

Title: The 3 Founders of Mahayana Buddhism in Singapore
Abstract: When Mahayana Buddhism arrived in Singapore, it was already more than a century later than the deeply entrenched polytheism beliefs practiced and embraced by the Hokian and Cantonese immigrant. The early Chinese immigrant population in the early 20th century enjoyed the liberty to practice the rituals associated with various religions and beliefs. Despite this, the learned venerables of Mahayana Buddhism, such as Venerable Zhuan Dao, Venerable Ci Hang, and Mr Li Juncheng, are able to establish their influence on the Chinese’s religious adherence in the midst of social anarchy, through their persistent evangelism at various opportunity and occasion. Although these Chinese Buddhist leaders in Singapore may not hold high the slogan of ’Humanistic Buddhism’, they have actively embarked on Buddhist social charity, education and cultural programs to realize the Buddhist Pure Land in Singapore. In sum, this paper analyses the means by which these three wise teachers of Mahayana Buddhism, who hail from China, sowed the seed and assisted in the germination, development and proliferation of Buddhism in Singapore.

Title: Local Concerns and Overseas Contacts of Singapore Mee Toh School
Abstract: Venerable Kong Hiap is one of the important Buddhist masters of Chinese Buddhism in Singapore. During learning the Dharma in southern Fujian, he had close fellowship with several eminent monks such as Ven. Taixu (1890-1947), Ven. Huiquan (1874-1942) and Ven. Hongyi (1880-1942). In 1937, Ven. Kong Hiap moved to Singapore to participate actively in the Chinese Buddhist sociey and also to promote the establishment of Mee Toh school after World War II. After 60 years of hard work, this school has become an excellent example of Mandrain studies and Buddhist education. Therefore, as looking at the development of Humanistic Buddhism in Singapore, it is nacessary to pay attention to the Buddhist education institution. This paper focuses on the development of Mee Toh School from the perspective of Buddhist education to analyzes its development process. Also I would like to discuss its influence on local education and charity activities in Singapore. In this paper, there are three parts: ’the establishment,’ ’local concerns,’ and ’overseas contacts’ to discuss how Chinese Buddhist communities in Singapore practice and disseminate the ideas of Humanistic Buddhism through Buddhist education.

Title: The Movement of Malaysian Buddhist Youth Under The Concept of Humanistic Buddhism by Master Hsing Yun
Abstract: This paper is to discuss the development, practice and achievements of the Buddhist Youth movement in Malaysia under the concept of Humanistic Buddhism by Master Hsing Yun. From here we explore how the concept of Humanistic Buddhism by master influence the Buddhist Youth movement in Malaysia. We discuss this from two aspects. The first one, Humanistic Buddhism and Buddhist Youth: The master’s attention to Buddhist Youth, Buddhist Youth movement led by master in Taiwan in his early stage, the cultivation of Buddhist Youth by Fo Guang Shan, and the activities of Buddhist youth to highlight the development of Humanistic Buddhism have inseparable relationship with Buddhist Youth; Secondly, Master Hsing Yun and early Buddhist Youth movement in Malaysia: Mainly describes the great influence and practice of Master’s Humanistic Buddhism concept on the early Buddhist Youth movement in Malaysia, this makes the Buddhist Youth movement in Malaysia develop vigorously. It also describes the establishment and practice of the Malaysia BLIA-YAD (Malaysia Buddha’s Light International Association - Young Adult Division) in Malaysia:We use the establishment of Fo Guang Shan Malaysia, BLIA Malaysia (Malaysia Buddha’s Light International Association) and BLIAYAD (Buddha’s Light International Association - Young Adult Division) in the 1990s as a background to describe how the concept of Humanistic Buddhism of Master Hsing Yun guides Malaysian Buddhist Youth and promotes the activities of Buddhism, and then implements and practices the concept of Master’s Humanistic Buddhism.

Title: Practice of Humanistic Buddhism for Social Care and Relief in East and Southeast Asia: a Case Study of Tianjin Buddhist Charitable Merit Foundation
Abstract: Firstly, this paper analyzes and expounds the related ideas of Master Taixu in carrying out the charity movement of humanistic Buddhism, and then combines with the examples of Buddhist charity activities of Tianjin Buddhist Charitable Merit Foundation in recent years. It also analyzes the ’the concept of modern charity’ summarized by Master Miao Xian, who is the leader of Tianjin Buddhist Charitable Merit Foundation. Lastly, it draws some inspirations to reflect on the present situation of the charity movement of humanistic Buddhism and to prospect its future.

Title: Master Hsing Yun’s Core Concept of Pure Land under His Vision of Humanistic Buddhismdhism
Abstract: Master Hsing Yun spares no effort to propagate ’Humanistic Buddhism.’ He advocates the return of the original intention and aspiration by which Buddha preached to his disciples about the benefit and joy associated with enlightenment. Moreover, he stresses the enlightenment brought about by dharma to this secular world, saying ’All preachments shall be construed as a part of Humanistic Buddhism as long as they contribute to the happiness of life.’ For Master Hsing Yun, Pure Land Buddhism is the backbone of his Humanistic Buddhism theorem as well as the most important part of his preachment and self-cultivation. In all Mahayana sutras, ’Pure Land of Ultimate Bliss’ is highly praised. Master Hsing Yun has introduced various modernistic thoughts such as ’Pure Land on the Earth’ and ’Pure Land Blessed with Buddha’s Light’ based on the thoughts of ’Pure Land of Ultimate Bliss’, attempting to enlighten all living creatures with different enlightening capacity in the world today. According to the disclosures made by Master Hsing Yun, ’Pure Land of Ultimate Bliss’ serves as a perfect example for Humanistic Buddhism whereas ’Pure Land on the Earth’ outlines the ultimate goal pursued by Humanistic Buddhism and ’Pure Land Blessed with Buddha’s Light’ is the tangible form of Humanistic Buddhism. As mentioned by the Master, ’Pure Land of Ultimate Bliss’, ’Pure Land on the Earth’ and ’Pure Land Blessed with Buddha’s Light’ undoubtedly serves as the core concept of Pure Land for Humanistic Buddhism. Master Hsing Yun stresses the practicality of dharma. He incorporates Pure Land thoughts into his preachment, attempting to enlighten the general public and thus construct a piece of pure land on the earth. He strives to develop the dharma system created by Fo Guang Shan in East Asia and in Southeast Asia into many pure and divine ritual sites featuring Pure Land, allowing monks and laymen to enjoy peacefulness and happiness no matter they are alive or deceased. Apparently, ’Pure Land of Ultimate bliss’, ’Pure Land on the Earth’ and ’Pure Land Blessed with Buddha’s Light’, as advocated by Master Hsing Yun, are tied to one another although they hint at something different from one another. It is therefore necessary to sort out their connotations and thereby find out their connections to Humanistic Buddhism.

Title: Humanistic Buddhism at Cross-Roads: Relevance of the Buddha’s Original Intent in Australia
Abstract: Venerable Master Hsing Yun promoted a form of Humanistic Buddhism that transformed Chinese Buddhism, limited to back alleys during much of the twentieth century, to become a part of mainstream Chinese-speaking Buddhist communities. In the face of declining Buddhist populations worldwide, Venerable Master Hsing Yun dedicated most of his life to promoting Buddhism through culture, education, charity and practice that complemented government programs. Following in the footsteps of the Buddha, his intent was also for every being to have faith in his or her compassion and kindness, thereby refraining from unwholesome acts and inspiring good deeds. As Humanistic Buddhism made its way into English-speaking countries with mature infrastructures, Fo Guang Shan adapted to these highly-educated and sophisticated societies, which preferred secularism or spirituality over religion (as could be seen from a growing atheist population) and unbundling over ’packaged deals’ (evidenced in the trend towards separating meditation from Buddhism and mindfulness from meditation). Nan Tien Temple has expanded the reach of Humanistic Buddhism to Australia over the past 25 years. This paper documents that journey through the example of the Buddha’s Birthday Festival held in Sydney’s iconic Darling Harbour over the past 20 years. It will identify issues related to the ’glocalising’ of a philosophy that has foreign roots. This paper will structure its discussions around possible intersections so that Humanistic Buddhism can remain relevant to people with different cultural backgrounds.

Title: Master Hsing Yun’s Interpretation of the Four Noble Truths and the Ideological Direction of Humanistic Buddhism
Abstract: This article explores Venerable Master Hsing Yun’s interpretation of the Four Noble Truths (catvāri āryasatyāni) in early Buddhism, and further correlates the Four Noble Truths to the Four Vast Vows in Mahayana Buddhism, and thereafter, inspect Venerable Master Hsing Yun’s ideological direction for Humanistic Buddhism. The research findings indicate that Venerable Master Hsing Yun believes that the Four Noble Truths is one of the most sacred canon, which is proven to provide an authentic way of experiencing life. Moreover, the sermon Buddha delivers in his life time (commonly known as ’turning the Wheel of Dharma’) can be relates to the later Four Noble Truths and the Four Vast Vows - both have shown ways to end suffering, thereby to let people moving on to a higher levels of existence, and finally to allow Humanistic Buddhism to attain the ideal of uniting mental comprehension and physical practice, and of the past and present.

Title: A Practice of Humanistic Buddhism in its Development in South East Asia - The Fo Guang Chan for Everyday Life
Abstract: The ’Fo Guang Chan for Everyday Life (Buddha’s Light Meditation Practice for Everyday Life)’ originated from Venerable Master Hsing Yun’s philosophy of ’Humanistic Buddhism’, which is the original intent of the Buddha. It formed the basis for daily practice of the later Chinese Southern School of Chan, in which ’the Buddha-Dharma can be realised in this world’. It also inherited the ’Human Life Buddhism’ philosophy proposed by Master Taixu in the early Republican era. In the last five decades, it was propagated and practiced actively by Master Hsing Yun and other prominent Buddhist masters, as well as by passionate Buddhist youths. It has gained popularity in Taiwan, and its influence is being spread gradually to other countries. The ’Fo Guang Chan for Everyday Life’ was established by Venerable Master Hsing Yun to meet the needs of modern day people. Being suitably adopted by urban citizens in their daily lives, it is a treatment for modern social problems, as it fulfils human physiological and psychological needs. It is also a concrete practice for one of the Fo Guang Shan objectives, which is ’To Purify Human Minds through Spiritual Cultivation’.

Title: The Idea of Peace and Practice of the Master Hsing Yun’s Humanistic Buddhism
Abstract: The life of the master Hsing Yun is committed to the promotion of humanistic Buddhism. His idea forms a unique concept of peace in the world of Buddhism and is used to guide practice in order to contribute to the safeguard of regional and world peace. The concept of peace of the master Hsing Yun is in the following aspects: first, is that ’benevolence’ is the fundamental law of Buddha, if everyone can treat all beings with compassion, so the world will be peace. second, is to promote the active concept and the method according to the fact; third, is actively promote the concept of pure land on earth. In its practice, the master Hsing Yun actively promotes the concept of peace of humanistic Buddhism. First of all the humanistic Buddhism of the master, Hsing Yun, advocated the spirit of being out of the world in order to serve the society. Secondly the master Hsing Yun, has always looked at the world with compassion, being concerned about regional disputes, ethnic conflicts, international conflicts of interest, cross-strait peace, and sensitive issues in today’s society, sensitive issues such as environmental protection, disease, network, cloning, euthanasia, organ donation and so on. The Buddhist concept of peace was the starting point of the master Hsing Yun, as propagates compassion, equality and tolerance, looking forward to the conflict mitigation and the contradiction between relaxation and harmony of all living beings. Last, the master Hsing Yun improvisioning insist on that the base of improving the value of modern people and adjusting the psychological desire is education, which is one of the important contents of the contemporary humanistic Buddhism for the society.

Title: On the Modern Mind-nature Theory and Practice Ways of Humanistic Buddhism
Abstract: Mind-nature theory is one of the core theories in Chinese Buddhism, humanistic Buddhism has a modern interpretation about the mind-nature theory which is different from the traditional Buddhism. The master Taixu and Yinshun made systematical interpretation and integration to the mind-nature theories of the various sectarians in Buddhism, so as to eliminate the differences in mind-nature theories , so the center task of Buddhist practices becomes how to promote Buddhism to adapt to modern society in the premise of insisting the fundamental Buddha teachings. Humanistic Buddhism mainly made modern interpretation of mind-nature theory in the following aspects: to improve the human nature and life as the focal point; emphasize social consciousness, combine the improvement of human nature with social transformation, renew the meanings of pure land and mind-nature; practice Buddha way in human experience; and so on. The modern interpretation of the mind-nature theory also determines that humanistic Buddhism has its obvious modern characteristics in the practice ways, namely, the nature of human, life, ethics, happiness, fusion and openness.

Title: On the Ecological Practice, Idea and Model of Humanistic Buddhism in Fo Guang Shan
Abstract: Humanistic Buddhism in Fo Guang Shan is a three-dimensional ecological system which integrates natural ecology, ecology of human mind and human ecology as a whole, and it takes man as the foremost, human mind as the core and the human world as the main scope. This paper discusses the ecological practice and historical changes of Fo Guang Shan from ’natural barren hill’ to ’the religious holy mountain’, the ecological idea and key guidance of Humanistic Buddhism from ’restoring nature’ to ’resuming human minds’, and the ecological pattern and spatial extension of Humanistic Buddhism in Fo Guang Shan from ’purifying public feeling’ to ’purifying the world of men’. The ecological practice, concept and model embody the unity of nature and humanity, the integration of wordly things and unwordly things, and the compatibility of the divine and the common. All these are the characteristics of Humanistic Buddhism in Fo Guang Shan. Therefore, the ecological practice of Humanistic Buddhism is not only the ecological practice of the natural pure land, but also the ecological practice of the idealist pure land, and at the same time, the ecological practice of pure land in the world.

Title: The Worship of Buddhist Relics and Contemporary Buddhism in South and Southeast Asia-As the Center of Relics of Contemporary Buddhist Dean Huan Presbyterian
Abstract: With the development of Buddhism in the world, especially in the context of globalization, the worship of relics were improving the local economic environment, and were enhancing the local cultural taste, and more important to local culture propagandized out. The sacred holy relics are releasing more halo, and are highlighting the special charm of Buddhism; before the Buddhist society was far from the world but now is return to the world, and become an important part of Buddhism, and caught the attention. The contemporary relics of Chinese monks’ were sent overseas countries out and built tower for collection and worship, and since the reform and opening-up, it is the most important turning point of the career of Humanistic Buddhism in Chinese Buddhism - The representative is the contemporary Buddhist Dean Huan Presbyterian. The contemporary Chinese first master, Zen sect and old Buddhist Dean, noble leader of Buddhism Huan Presbyterian, and relics of his showed in the public and was sent to the foreign countries to worship; it shows the position of China Buddhism in contemporary world Buddhism, and Influence in World Buddhist range, to improve the China Buddhist community has an important role in promoting the world Buddhist status, also has an important impact on the propaganda of Chinese Buddhism.

Title: Study in Fo Guang Shan Humanistic Buddhism Theory of Practice
Abstract: Master Hsing founder of Fo Guang Shan, the Promotion of human Buddhism, with a large number of students and followers. He explained in a simple and easy way, make people understand and hold various kinds of activities, let the students and followers influenced imperceptibly by Buddhism. The thesis is based on the method of practice imparted by the master of the Hsing Yun, The theory of Buddhist practice in Fo Guang Shan Buddhism Humanistic Buddhism. First, carry forward the eight sects ,Second, understanding and Practice ,Third, compassion and wisdom.

Title: Philosophical Approaches to Modern Interpretation of ’Humanistic Buddhism’ and Its Reflection
Abstract: This paper examines the process of the philosophical approach in the modern interpretation of the thought of ’Humanistic Buddhism’ and sums up the three characteristics of the modern research paradigm of ’Buddhism’, which is initiated by Xiong Shili, The positive significance and important contribution of the philosophical approach of Buddhism is that the philosophical approach demonstrates a sufficient reason for the existence of traditional Buddhism in the present society, but also inevitably causes a certain degree of the integrity and real interest of Buddhist thought damage. ’Humanistic Buddhism’ in the further development of the East Asian region, the philosophical approach to the interpretation of the next step in the depth of Buddhism, all depends on the establishment of Buddhism in the modern society of the subjectivity and promotion.

Title: The Thoughts of Humanistic Buddhism in the Field of Jianzhen Spirit
Abstract: After six hard tryings on travelling by sea and finally having done it, Master Jianzhen took Japan with Chinese traditional cultures like Buddhist discipline, carving, architecture, paiting, music, medicine, etc. His experiences and activities are all samples of the practice of ’Humanisctic Budhhism’, although he left few written mentions on these issues. This article would like to make some understandings on Jianzhen’s thoughts of ’Life for Buddhism’ from his education and his studens as well as his Buddhist practices, so as to make us Buddhists to follow Jianzhen’s thoughts and fulfill Humanisctic Budhhism, encouraging us to practice Vinaya, Pancavidya, Tripitaka with diligence, tenacity, no-cowardice and no-retrograding. We will realise our Buddhist studying and cultivating from the beginning to the destination by this way and requite favours to Triple Gems and sentient human beings as a result.

Title: Egalitarianism in Humanistic Buddhism and its Impact on Contemporary Buddhism
Abstract: Buddhism has long been a strong advocate for egalitarianism between the four groups of Buddhist disciples. Nevertheless, in a patriarchal society, equality does not come naturally - the idea that only a monk can promote Buddhist is indeed a misconception formed in the era which the dharma ends (sad-dharma-vipralopa). Under the hierarchical order of the sangha, inequality is still prevalent in Buddhist organizations. Venerable Master Hsing Yun is a relentless advocate for equality in his works, and has successfully integrated such values between genders, age groups, monks and laymen in the foundation and culture of Fo Guang Shan Monastery. ’Oneness and coexistence’ is one of the core virtues that Master Hsing Yun promotes in Humanistic Buddhism. The actualization of ’oneness and coexistence’ is the embodiment of equality, which is likewise Buddha’s original intent. With his book Humanistic Buddhism: Holding True to the Original Intents of Buddha as the theme, this article will explore how the idea of gender equality is realized in Fo Guang Shan under the leadership of Venerable Master Hsing Yun, and its effect on contemporary Buddhism.

Title: The Development of Modern Buddhism in Korea and the Influence of the Master Hsing Yu
Abstract: The reform process of modern Korean Buddhism can be roughly divided into two stages: the early modern period and the modern period. In the early modern period, The Jogye Order, who advocated keeping the traditions of the deaf, had a debate with The Taego Order. The results of this movement caused The Taego Order to be greatly weakened The Jogye Order became the largest Buddhist corps in Korea. In the modern era, Christianity and Catholicism actively participated in the construction of social welfare and conducted religious communication by serving the society. Because of the conservative thinking and the lack of active response, the Korean Buddhist corps caused a significant decrease in the number of believers. To cope with this trend, the Buddhist world began to learn from the experience of spreading Buddhism overseas. Master Hsing Yun traveled to South Korea as early as 1975. The master’s human ideology of Buddhism has played an important role in the development of modern Buddhism in Korea. Seoul Fo Guang Shan is the first Buddhist temple established by a foreign Buddhist group in Korea. Many years after the establishment of the Fo Guang Shan in Seoul, Buddhist groups such as Thailand, Burma, Sri Lanka, and Mongolia began to establish their temples in Korea. Chinese Confucian culture affects all aspects of Korean society, and Buddhism is no exception. How can humanistic Buddhism cherish its results in Korea, and it requires more reflection from within Korean Buddhism than from the outside.

Title: From ’People-oriented’ to ’Oneness and Coexistence’ - a brief discussion about Venerable Master Hsing Yun’s view towards animal rights
Abstract: In the research field of Humanistic Buddhism, there are very few discussions over animal rights. In fact, Buddhist teachings could be very contributive to the progression of animal rights. Under such circumstance, this article intends to reveal Venerable Master Hsing Yun’s view towards animal rights. Ven. Master Hsing Yun’s idea over animal protection is indeed a unique one when compare to existing discussions. According to present studies, Buddhist views over animal protection could be categorized into two stances theoretically, including: 1) People-oriented, and 2) Equality of All Sentient Beings. From rigorous academic point of view, both stances have their limitations and rooms for improvement. For instance, would emphasizing ’people-oriented’ contain a kind of anthropocentric value? When we think about that all sentient beings are equal and sympathize the living condition of animals, does it imply that humankind has a higher status comparing with other animals? This article suggests that Ven. Master Hsing Yun’s idea over animal protection is a breakthrough from these previous views and does not face such limitations. Starting from the stance of ’people-oriented’, Ven. Master Hsing Yun also emphasizes on the value of ’unity between people (and other existences)’; thus, eventually reach the conclusion of ’oneness and coexistence’. To sum up, Ven. Master Hsing Yun has provided us a more comprehensive theory about animal protection. The aim of this article is to reveal and explain the idea of Ven. Master Hsing Yun in a systematic way.

Title: The discussion of Master Hsing Yun’s relief theory and its practice
Abstract: Master Hsing Yun puts the humanistic Buddhism thought and theory into practice. His promoting modern Buddhism should carry forward the traditional spirit of relief, and in the order to combine a relief spirit of Buddhist with the actual world preferably, which shows a brand new concept of relief. Master Hsing Yun draws the spirit of ’Compassion and happiness’ of Bodhisattva of Mahayana, as the theoretical construction of his relief concept. While Master Hsing Yun stresses ’relief of other strengths’, he advocates the propagating of the Buddhist service and devotion idea in the form of certain architecture, with the practicing of Buddhism’s relief concept, and shall be in this method of suitable opportunity. Master HsingYun also takes full advantage of the advanced information facilities in the new era. He inherits and reforms the traditional way of propagating, and carries out an equitable relief with a series of ’human Buddhism movement’. These relief practices transcend national boundaries and spread all over the world, showing the ’equal and undifferentiated’ relief concept of the Master Hsing Yun.

Title: The Spread and Promotion of Master Hsing Yun’s Humanistic Buddhism in Malaysia From The Aspect of Culture and Education
Abstract: This article discusses the cultural and educational undertakings from two aspects, the Humanistic Buddhism of Fo Guang Shan and Fo Guang Shan in Malaysia. These two approaches bring along few issues. Firstly, why Fo Guang Shan emphasize on the cultural and educational undertakings and put in lots of resources to the development of cultural and educational undertakings. Secondly, how does the development of cultural and educational undertakings being promoted under Master Hsing Yun’s concept on Humanistic Buddhism. Thirdly, the importance role of cultural and educational undertakings in the propagation of Humanistic Buddhism. Fourthly, as case study of Fo Guang Shan in Malaysia, how’s the condition and achievement in the promotion of cultural and educational undertakings. Through the study and discussion of the above issues, the motives and methods in promoting cultural and educational undertakings by Fo Guang Shan can be known better. Besides that, the characteristic and the condition of development of cultural and educational undertakings in abroad can be determined as well. If the promotion of cultural and educational undertakings is the skillful mean in the propagation of Humanistic Buddhism by Fo Guang Shan, the humanistic concern that brought along, will be the goal in promoting social harmony, purification of mind and establishment of pure land in this world. This is aligned with the teaching in Mahayana Buddhism which emphasize on ’explain the teaching to bring benefit and joy’.

Title: Humanistic Buddhism in the eyes of the Japanese- with special focus on FoGuang Shan
Abstract: Led by the Venerable Master Hsing Yun, Fo Guang Shan and its counterpart, the Buddha’s Light International Association, have contributed greatly to the practice and preaching of Humanistic Buddhism, expanding its influence in the past decades. Since 1990, the influence of Fo Guang Shan has spread to Japan, where numerous Fo Guang Shan monasteries have been founded, and the publications of Venerable Master Hsing Yun and Fo Guang Shan have been introduced. However, till today, there are no study on Fo Guang Shan dharma preaching in Japan and its influence, as well as the way the Japanese regard and react to Fo Guang Shan and its preaching on Humanistic Buddhism. In fact, there are very little material of reference on the topic can be found. In order to fill the research gap in this aspect, this paper introduces the main research results, basic viewpoints and unique perspectives of Fo Guang Shan and Humanistic Buddhism according to the relevant papers of Japanese scholars.

Title: Verification on Relationship in Conceptualization of ’Humanistic Buddhism’ and Theory of ’Buddha is a human’
Abstract: Basing on the thought about ’Mahayana Buddhism, non-Buddha’s doctrine’(大乘非佛說) and ideas of ’New Buddhism Movement’(新佛教運動), recent scholars in Japan put forward the theory of ’Buddha is a human’(佛陀人間論), in which theory exploration, has high similar characteristic to ’Humanistic Buddhism’( 人間佛教) started by subsequent Chinese monks such as Master Taixu、Master Yinshun and so on. It was lacking material evidences about whether ’Humanistic Buddhism’ in China was influenced by thought of Buddhism from Japan or not, However, one fundamental fact could be realized is that, as a pioneer, Master Taixu maybe have some understanding about the theory of ’Buddha on Earth’ in his early life. Master Taixu preferred to adopt the concept of ’Humanistic Buddhism’, which is very likely due to different distinct understanding about ’Earth’ in ’Buddha on Earth ’in Chinese and Japanese.

Title: Manjushri Method in the early Mahayana and Salvation spirit of Humanistic Buddhism
Abstract: The proposition and practice of Humanistic Buddhism have transformed the spirit of of Buddhism which emphasis ghosts and death. Buddhism has got rid of its old colors and enriched itself with the salvation spirit. In this process of practice, Buddhism has received universal attention from both the monks and the worldly people. The connotation of Humanistic Buddhism lies in the pursuit of becomeing Buddha in this world. How to understand the Bodhisattva theory is very important to Humanistic Buddhism. This paper will take the example of Bodhisattva theory revealed by the early Mahayana scriptures and explore the positive value of the Manjushri method.

Title: The implementation of Theravadic concepts into Social Actions
Abstract: Buddhism has always been socially engaged, because it addresses human suffering and is searching for solutions and methods to overcome it. Like the Buddha 2,500 years ago, humanistic Buddhism today seeks liberation of all beings from the illusion of separation. In this world torn by conflicts and devastating wars, it is impossible to pretend that suffering is not happening somewhere out there, to somebody ’other’, who is radically different from us. The understanding of co-responsibility and interconnectedness could be and should be evoked by humanistic Buddhism. The paper presents a Buddhist philosophical background for conflict transformation, which can be applicable to different social settings, taking into account the diversity of sociocultural conditions. The paper will (de)construct the phases of identity formation on the basis of the concept of paticca-samuppāda (dependent origination) and anatta (non-self). In Buddhist understanding, conflict is the result of defensiveness and misconceptions, and thus it is central to understanding the mechanism by which the idea of ’I’ or ’self ’ is established. The purpose of humanistic Buddhism should be (among other things) to achieve a radical change in perception, which leads to ’de-automatization’ of mental mechanisms and suspends identification with sensory and mental experiences that an individual calls a separate ’I’. With the understanding of co-responsibility and interconnectedness, humanistic Buddhism can offer a powerful tool for protecting lives of all sentient beings as well as change the attitude toward environmental issues and raise ecological awareness not just in East Asia and Southeast Asia, but globally.

Title: Where is the Human Realm? An Examination of Ven. Taixu’s 太虛大師 Concept of Renjian 人間 in his Pure Land Writings
Abstract: Ven. Taixu ( 太虛大師, 1890~ 1947) is justly credited with initiating the idea of the modern Buddhist movement called renjian fojiao 人間佛教, variously known in English as ’Humanistic Buddhism,’ ’Engaged Buddhism,’ ’Earthly Buddhism,’ and ’Buddhism for the Human Realm.’ Scholars and followers of this movement generally understand this as a movement which emphasizes the present life. It seeks to motivate Buddhists to take actions that will promote social welfare and human flourishing while discouraging an excessive ’other-worldliness’ (chushi 出世). In other words, Buddhists should strive to improve life here and now and not focus their attention on the afterlife or on other worlds. While Taixu’s writings do indeed contain such calls to action, a closer examination of his works dealing with the creation of the ’Pure Land in the Human Realm’ (renjian jingtu 人間淨土) reveals his concept of the ’human realm’ to be far more complex than this. In addition to calling for concrete actions to educate and uplift people in society, he acknowledges the ongoing reality of death and advises practices aimed at a better afterlife. In addition, in defining the ’human realm’ he relies on traditional Buddhist cosmology more than is generally acknowledged. For example, the Buddhist scriptures say that human beings, as one of the six paths of rebirth, are found not only on planet Earth but also in the northern continent of Uttarakuru and in the Pure Land of Amitābha. Thus, translations such as ’Earthly Pure Land’ fail to capture his full meaning, and modern interpretations of his ideas on Humanistic Buddhism imagine him as more of a modernizer than he actually is. This paper will look to Taixu’s major statement on the Pure Land in the Human Realm, the essay ’On the Establishment of the Pure Land in the Human Realm’ (’Jiànshè rénjiān jìngtǔ lùn ’ 建設人間淨土論 ) as well as other writings on Pure Land to present the full complexity of Taixu’s thought. It will argue that he is more of a transitional figure and less a fully-committed modernizer than has been recognized.

Title: A research in Master Fafang’s propagating activities in India
Abstract: Master Fafang (1904~1951) is one of the disciples of Master Taixu. He has a high status in modern Chinese Buddhism history due to his profound knowledge and deep researching. He taught in Buddhist Academy of Wuchang, Bailin Dogma of Buddhism and Han-Tibet Dogma of Buddhism. He also took charge of the World Foxueyuan Library. Haichaoyin was issued under his general editorship for three times. He positively practiced the World Buddhism Movement and the thought of Humanistic Buddhism which was proposed by Master Taixu. These acts made him a positive practiser in Humanistic Buddhism and Buddhist Education. He was good at English, Sanskrit, Pali and Japanese. He also has a good understanding of Chinese Buddhism, Tibetan Buddhism and Southern Buddhism, took an active part in domestic and international Buddhism scholars. Master Fafang accepted the entrustment of Master Taixu-developing and expanding China’s Buddhism in the other part of the world. He tried his best expanding the thought of Buddhism all over the world, especially in South-East Asia and South Asia. Most of all, he made a great contribution in striving for foreign aid to support the War of Resistance Against Japan. Master Fafang once taught in India for two times. During his staying in India, he communicated with Indian monks, researched Southern Buddhism, translated Sutra, taught Chinese Buddhism and culture. In these ways he opened a new era of the rejuvenation in Chinese Mahayana. He devoted in promoting Sangha Education and dispatching monks to go to India for studying and communicating. He used every chances to propagate the situation of China’s anti-Japan War, exposed the crimes of Japanese invaders, for instance, destroying Chinese temples and Buddhism culture. He appealed Buddhist all over the world getting together to carry forward the spirit of peace-loving and fight against the aggressive war along with its destruction towards Buddhist culture.

Title: On Master Hsing Yun’s Aesthetics of Nature
Abstract: Master Hsing Yun’s aesthetics of nature is mainly reflected in three aspects. One is the idea of ’heaven and earth are both writing’, which implies the beauty of wuyu . From the language of nature----wuyu, humans can experience and reflect. Master Hsing Yun advocates humans’ being close to nature so as to broaden their mind and understand the art of learning. The beauty of wuyu also lies in its aesthetic manner of ’viewing objects in terms of objects’. The second is the idea that all human beings are born equal. Master Hsing Yun always shows mercy on animals and advocates harmony between man and nature. The third is Master Hsing Yun’s view on the relationship between Zen and nature, i.e. there is a connection between Zen and the beauty of nature. Influenced by Buddhist thought such as three marks of existence, Theory of Origin, ’all of Dharmadhatu come from our mind’, a concept of equality, Zen thought and Humanistic Buddhism, Master Hsing Yun’s aesthetics of nature manifests a particular connotation.

Title: The Theoretical Inheritance and Practice Exposition of the Humanistic Buddhism:An Interpretive Expositive Way from Tiantai Zhiyito Fo Guang Shan Hsing Yun
Abstract: Tiantai Zhiyi judged the Buddha’s insightful teachings in Wonderful Dharma Lotus Sutra as the Complete Teaching at the 5th periods, and pointed out all sentient beings can be enlightened into the Buddha’s wisdom, and there is no difference between them and the Buddha proclaimed in Wonderful Dharma Lotus Sutra . Nothing else is the edification of Buddhism Venerable Master Hsing Yun Advocates Buddhism has to be the Humanistic Buddhism and emphasizes what the Humanistic Buddhism is the origins of Buddhism. And that’s the reason Wang Lixing, the publisher of Tien-Xia Groups, praises the origins of Hsing Yun as ’wisdom and compassion transcending religions’. In this paper, the author describes at first how Tiantai Zhiyi valued the origins of Buddhism in Wonderful Dharma Lotus Sutra and the life care and humanistic practice of the Tiantai Teachings and Meditation. Secondly, the author analyzes the Humanistic Buddhism promoted by Venerable Master Hsing Yun as the origins of Buddhism. According to the comparison of specific expositions between both approaches, the author demonstrates the theoretical contexts, practical expositions and the historical heritages of the Humanistic Buddhism.

Title: Master Ci Hang’s Dhama and Cultural Propagation in Burma
Abstract: Master Ci Hang visited Burma and went to worship the Great Golden Stupa in November 1930. Next year he studied at Chinese Tripitaka office in Yangon. And then he established ’the Chinese Buddhism Society’ there, which became a base to implement Master Tai Xu’s Humanistic Buddhism. Since July 1932 he had initiated Humanistic Buddhism propagation with writing works. Before back to China in January 1936, he had published at least four books which included ’Buddhist Three Characters Sutra for Children’, ’Light Explanation of Buddhist Three Characters Sutra for Children’, ’Notes for Propriety’, and ’ New Editing Significant Chanting Compilation’. In August 1936 , ’Master Ci Hang Speech Compilation’ was edited by Shu Qing and then published by the WU HAN Press. This book included all his popular speech content while he was at the Chinese Buddhist Society in Burma and within half an year right after back to China. Master Ci Hang had spent five years in Burma and gained countless Buddhism propagation achievements there. After back to China, his constantly giving speeches everywhere had showed more his patriotic ideal through Buddhism.

Title: Rethink on the Chinese Translation of ’Le bouddhisme engagé’: among Modern Buddhism, Socialism and Leftist in Thailand
Abstract: A decade ago, I translated the French term ’Le bouddhisme engagé’ and its English version ’Socially / Engaged Buddhism’ into Chinese, literally as ’Leftist / Left-wing Buddhism’ (zuǒ yì fó jiāo) . I will recosider the suitability of the translation in this article. The article is composed of four sections. Firstly, Thailand is taken as the key reference in this article to consider the relation between Buddhism and public engagement, leftist and socialism. Secondly, Le bouddhisme engagé will be examined under the multiple encounters between Buddhism and socialism, through which different models can be developed. Thirdly, various types of ’Right-wing Buddhism’, theopponent of Left-wing Buddhism (zuǒ yì fó jiāo, Le bouddhisme engagé) will be discussed to illuminate the basic natures of Le bouddhisme engagé. Both of them are the response to the modernity although their positions are profoundly different. Fourthly, to discuss whether the reformist Thai monk Buddhadāsa Bhikkhu (1906~1993 A.D.)’s Dhammic Socialism (Thai: Dhammika Sangkomniyom) should be considered as part of Thailand’s Left-wing Buddhism or Le bouddhisme engagé. The final remark will argue that the Chinese version for Le bouddhisme engagé / Engaged Buddhism should be reconsidered according to the above examples.

Title: A Study of Master Xingyun thought on Mind-only Pure Land
Abstract: As Fo Guang Shan mountains were long, the grand master Xingyun to carry forward Buddhism as its mission. In the years of practice of Dharma in the grand master Xingyun, formed its own unique idea of pure land. In the eyes of the Venerable Master Hsing Yun, construction of Humanistic Buddhism to implement is to construct the idealist pure land, we in this Saha world change to clean and bright the idealism of the pure land. Master Hsing Yun in explaining his idealist pure land thought, theory of consciousness only ’three realms idealism’ and ’law consciousness’ as a supporting point for the their idealism in the pure land thought, will also the Vimalakirti ’the idealism of the pure land claims be combined with a, and the Platform Sutra of the sixth patriarch for the theory of land to be played, all of these become the venerable master to carry forward the theoretical basis of idealism in the pure land. Also Master Hsing Yun thinks, to change the evil world of five turbidities idealist pure land, then you need to starting from the purification of the soul, that is mentioned in the Buddhist scriptures in idealist pure land theory. Adhering to this philosophy, Master Hsing Yun according to which he created the Fo Guang Shan Dharma model, specifically elaborated the issue how to build the idealist pure land.

Title: Humanistic Pure Land and Amitābha Pure Land
Abstract: Buddhism is human oriented. This implies, the focus of Humanistic Buddhism is on humanity. As the Platform Sutra says, ’Buddhism appears in this world. Awareness separates never from this world.’ The contemporary society is of group organization. If one wants to survive and be respected in the group, he has to benefit himself and in the meantime to benefit the group. By doing so, one can earn the recognition. Hence, in order to fit into the modern world, Buddhism has to be capable of engaging to Rationality and to Conditionality. What people need is deeds that benefit the group. Therefore, liberation has to be gained through the service to the public, and only through the great Compassion and great Wisdom of Mahayana Buddhism, together with the spirit of ’Four dharmas of attraction’ and ’Sympathetic Joy, Compassion, Empathetic Joy, Equanimity’. Buddhism has to seek a starting point in contemporary life. The preach of the practice thus becomes: first to achieve the completeness of personality. That is, ’only from the Buddha comes the learning; through the achievement of personality comes the completion.’ By then, one can further develop ’the superhuman, transcend the superhuman, into the subtlety, to the unlimited state.’ Thus, contemporary Buddhism can start with precepts and benevolent acts, and with purgatory of all the sufferings in daily life together with their causes. With the cessation of the suffering, one could further achieve the completion of awareness.

Title: Master Xingyun’s Recent Thoughts on Humanistic Buddhism with special focus on the Idea of Humanistic Pure Land
Abstract: The efforts made by Master Xingyun for promoting Humanistic Buddhism are noticeable by all, the trend of Buddhist movement is no less than a school of Chinese Buddhism. Regardless how the public looks at the system and activities of the Foguang Shan, as only time could tell if they could accept Humanistic Buddhism for the long run. The important thing for us to do is to write down the principle and theory of the Foguang Shan’s operation, leaving records for future generation. Thus, studies of the master’s thought at his different life phases are important writing materials for Humanistic Buddhism practiced at the Foguang Shan. This author holds that the master’s efforts in promoting peace across the straights represents his Buddhist wisdom of going along with the time and causes and conditions of the situation.

Title: Some thoughts about the future development of the Humanistic Buddhism: with a reflection on the idea of the ’Pure Land’
Abstract: Humanistic Buddhism was originated from Master Tai Xu, and become a trend in contemporary Buddhism as a result of the cultivation and development by Master Yin Shun. In Taiwan as well as in the Chinese mainland, the idea of Humanistic Buddhism is an important issue that cannot be ignored by scholarly communities or by the Buddhist circle. In the past, the author has published an article on the Humanistic Buddhism, elaborating its origins, theory, promotion, definition, and future development. The article argues that although contemporary Humanistic Buddhism seems to be popular and prevailing, there are also many implicit problems awaiting resolution. This article is based on my previous research, with a special focus on the future development of the Humanistic Buddhism. It also seeks to explore the best way to substantiate the idea of ’Pure Land.’ This article is divided into the following sections: Master Tai Xu’s perceptions about the Humanistic Buddhism; some thoughts about the future direction of the Humanistic Buddhism; and the relationship between Humanistic Buddhism and Pure Land. It is the author’s hope that this article will elaborate the problems and dilemmas confronting Humanistic Buddhism, and provide some possible ways to settle these problems and dilemmas.

Title: The Pure Land Thought in Contemporary Southern Vietnam
Abstract: The So-called Humanistic Pure Land means to improve or build a better society with the spirit of Engaged Buddhism or Buddhism in Human world. In Vietnam, Humanistic Buddhism is much stressed, and Buddhism has a great influence on society and the state. ever since the ancient time (111BCE) when Buddhism just entered Vietnam, Buddhist Humnistic thought had been there. Buddhism can’t exist without mankind, its flourished and declined with the country. Buddhist temples are built in hustle cities, monastics are not recluse hiding in the forest, but live with common people, sharing suffering and happiness. with the development society, Engaged Buddhism changed too: Buddhism in Vietnam no is not only believed in temples offering ritual services to the public but also practiced rather than preached in words. Intended to serve society, Vietnamese Buddhism has crossed the boundaries of different occupations, from charity to relief service, to education, to counseling and even to government. Vietnamese Buddhism and society together striving to build Huamnistic Pure Land, with action showing the relationship between Buddhism and the secular world, just like what is said in the Platform Sutra, 」 Being in the world, Buddhism guides people of the world, without the world, there will not be enlightenment.

Title: Humanistic Buddhism in the Digital Age: Make Truth Great Again!
Abstract: NoAbstract

Title: The Flowering of the Academic Study of Humanistic Buddhism in the West: The Institute for the Study of Humanistic Buddhism at University of the West
Abstract: NoAbstract

Title: Discussing the temples of Buddhist are changed process from tradition to modernization in Taiwan,for example,Fo Guang Shan.
Abstract: In contemporary society, Buddhist temples must continuously adapt their architecture and spatial design to meet the evolving needs of the time and societal changes. By exploring the development of Buddhist architecture across different periods, from India to China and Taiwan, the spatial forms of Buddhist buildings have undergone historical transformations, then clearly define the nature and function of traditional temple space. This research aims to examine how the characteristics of traditional temple spaces are manifested in a new manner under the context of modernization, focusing on the influence of Humanistic Buddhism on Buddhist architecture. Humanistic Buddhism emphasizes the integration of Buddhist teachings into everyday life through spatial design, allowing Buddhist wisdom to be incorporated into people’s daily lives. Taking Fo Guang Shan as an example, this research investigates how Master Hsing Yun modernized traditional temple spaces by emphasizing spatial functionality and practicality, enabling the temples to better meet the needs of contemporary society. The study explores emerging spaces within the Fo Guang Shan temple complex. In addition to considerations of modernization, the study also explores how to create a sacred atmosphere in modern temple buildings. By synthesizing the above content, this thesis aims to analyze how Master Hsing Yun’s philosophy of Humanistic Buddhism is implemented in the spatial design of Fo Guang Shan temple, combining tradition and modernity, and creating a sacred ambiance within the temple spaces. Through the study of Fo Guang Shan, it is hoped that this research can provide insights and references for future studies and Improved Buddhist temple buildings, achieving compatibility between tradition and modernity.

Title: Environment in Taiwanese Humanistic Buddhism: The Case of the Tzu Chi Foundation 1990-2021
Abstract: The purpose of this thesis is to research the interactions between Buddhism and environmentalism within one of the biggest and most successful Buddhist organizations in Taiwan: the Tzu Chi Foundation. I argue that Tzu Chi has integrated environmental discoursesand practices within Buddhism. The adoption of environmentalism responds to its rise to prominence due to worsening natural conditions. Environmentalism offers legitimacy in times of uncertainty. This allows Tzu Chi to adapt to a new grammar and retain its power as a religious organization. In order to back up my claims, I have gathered desk literature related to Buddhism and environmentalism. In addition, I analysed Tzu Chi primary sources, such as documents and videos, and carried out fieldwork, visiting one of the Tzu Chi recycling centres.

Title: Faith and Practice of The Buddhist Laity ： A Case Study of Daksinavana Bhiksuni Samgha Ashram
Abstract: The Buddhist religious practice in modern Taiwan has been spreading out of temples with the trend of diversification.Among the current sects of Buddhism, sangha transformation, the prevailing humanistic Buddhism philosophy, and the vinaya reforms of female practitioners, Daksinavana Bhiksuni Samgha Ashram, located in central Taiwan, still persists in restoring the vinaya of ancient Indian Buddhism, and promoting the practice of upholding precepts and chanting Amitabha Buddha.However, with only a group of Sangha who adhere to the Buddhist precepts, Buddhism cannot be promoted well. It also requires concordance and interaction of both Sangha and laity, the full support of laity is needed to revitalize Buddhism. The intention of this study is to understand the practice in monasteries specializing in vinaya and Pure Land, and the interaction between Sangha and laity. The aim is to understand the self-realization of laity with qualitative research method. The object of this study are the laity who fully support monasteries specializing in vinaya and Pure Land. After recognizing the practices in monasteries specializing in vinaya and Pure Land, contemporary Buddhist laity observe the interactions between sangha and laity, to understand how sangha guide laity to utilize the concepts of precepts to support sangha, monasteries, and Buddhism, with vinaya as the main issue. Support is an important medium between bhikkhunis and laity. Bhikkhunis and laity work together based upon mutual belief. Though the collaboration, they not only fulfill their religious achievements respectively, but also revitalize Buddhism. Furthermore, religion can be not only spiritually reliable for people in the current life, but also bring endless hope to the people who believe in past, present, and future. On the other hand, the essence of vinaya education is to keep mental precepts. People spiritually purify themselves, with pure behavior, pure talks, and pure mind, to implement Buddhist doctrine in their social life. If one intends to practice, self-cultivation is needed; many precepts must be implemented, such as physically, having no vicious behavior, and verbally, performing no criticism, no flattery, no divisive talk, and no lying.Like Confucianism, which says people who want to cultivate their behaviors need to cultivate their mind, people need to follow secular moral standards themselves, then promote these Buddhist precepts in society in order to purify civilization.By following religious doctrine, people can purify their minds and live harmoniously in a stable society. From a positive perspective, we confirm relevant main ideas and outlines of the influence between the development of vinaya education and correct views.

Title: Self-identity Formation of LGB in Relation to Religious Belief: Christians and Buddhists as Examples
Abstract: Spirituality is a basic factor in human existence. In this thesis, we studied the self-identity formation of homosexuals in relation to their religious belief, taking Christians and Buddhists as examples. Through qualitative research, in-depth interviews were carried out with four Christians and four Buddhists, including lesbians, gays, and bisexuals (LGB). The results show how religious belief affects the identity of LGB, and the impact from their significant others, like religious group members, companions, and family, as they participate in religious activities in sangha or church. For the Christian LGB, we found that the timing in contact with their religious belief was the main factor affecting their self-identity formation. Therefore, we classified these processes into two categories: an LGB born in a Christian family and the one who was the very first Christian in their family. As for the Buddhist LGB, the religious belief had less influence on their self-identity formation; their development of identity mainly depended on the ways they practiced the Buddhist doctrine as well as the different sangha in which they were involved. Thus another two categories were utilized to classify the Buddhist LGB: the LGB in traditional Buddhism sangha and the LGB in a humanistic Buddhism sangha. In this study, we found that almost all of the participants had discovered their sexual orientation in their teens, i.e. they were more attracted to the same sex. In this stage, some Christians start to develop homophobia due to their religious beliefs; conversely, the Buddhist LGBs did not have such attitudes generally. However, both groups may have become confused as to whether they were LGBs, and thus sought for assistance from their friends to resolve their doubts. After extending their relationship to people with similar sexual orientation, or even developed into an intimate relationship with some of them, they would go on to develop their identity, and try to combine their religious identity and LGB identity. This process would vary with church/sangha, family, companions and their attitudes to such issue. For instance, among our research participants, some devoted Christian LGBs decided to change their sexual orientation to satisfy the expectation of their God, while the others chose to hold onto their beliefs but refused to participate in church activities. On the other hands, the Buddhist LGBs in Buddhism sangha placed more emphasis on the opinions in Buddhist texts than that of the teachers they had converted under. For the LGBs belonging to a humanist Buddhism sangha, the opinion of their teacher was somewhat more important than those recorded in Buddhist texts. Based on the research findings, we suggest that the professional helpers should take personal religious beliefs into consideration when assisting an LGB who is “coming out’. Moreover, the helpers should also note if there is family violence associated with their religion. Finally, we recommend that the religious organizations and their leaders should lend an ear to the LGBs and try to better understand their lives.

Title: Exploring Corporate Management and Corporate Social Responsibility under the Concept of “Environmental Protection of the Mind’ -- Case Studies on Fellow Members of DDM Dharma Practice Society
Publisher: Dharma Drum Institute of Liberal Arts
Abstract: Master Sheng Yen was a religious leader with great wisdom and vision. He saw how ordinary people had tended to focus on improving material comforts in life but overlooked the importance of self-growth from within. These days many have realized that, through endless consumption of natural resources and production of wastes, human have done great damage on mother nature. To repair this and to protect the natural environment for future generations and other species on earth, we must first be willing to examine and modify our fundamental values. It is to the benefit of all if we value more on the wellbeing of the whole than on individual gains; more on the long run impact than on immediate success. Only so, a better world for all can be possibly attained. Master Sheng Yen founded Dharma Drum Mountain (DDM) in 1989 only to dedicate it to the practice and promotion of “Humanistic Buddhism’, emphasis of which is placed on the practice of a Bodhisattva path in this present world. He later initiated the concept of “Spiritual Environmentalism’ in 1992, which has since become the core concept of DDM in moving forward with its vision of “Uplifting the Character of Humanity and Building a Pure Land on Earth’. The term “Spiritual Environmentalism’ was created by Master Sheng Yen based on the essence of Vimalakirti Sutra and Chan teachings for ease of comprehension by modern people. It is the core among “The Four Kinds of Environmentalism’ advocated by him, namely: Spiritual Environmentalism, Natural Environmentalism, Living Environmentalism, and Social Environmentalism. Master Sheng Yen further formulated complete methodologies that are easy to understand and practical to implement as guides for applying the concept of “Spiritual Environmentalism’ in daily life. The so named “The Five Fourfold Practices of the Mind’ and “The Six Ethics of the Mind’ had been clearly defined and thoroughly expounded by Master Sheng Yen throughout the years before he passed away in 2009. DDM Dharma Practice Society (DDMDPS) is one of several sub-organizations of DDM. The sole qualification required to join DDMDPS membership is the completion of DDM’s 3-day Social Elites Meditation Retreat (renamed as Self-Transcendence Chan Meditation Retreat in 2009). Therefore, members of DDMDPS consist of elites from all professional fields. Individually or collectively they are in the position to bring about greater impact onto society. DDMDPS’s mission is to help spread the benefits of practicing Dharma, and to advance DDM’s vision of “Uplifting the Character of Humanity and Building a Pure Land on Earth’. Entering the 18th year since its founding in 1999, members of DDMDPS have gradually matured in their pursuit of a Buddhist faith and in their devotion to the Bodhisattva path. They have deepened their comprehension of Dharma as well as experience in Chan meditation through consistent involvement in DDM Dharma teachings and activities. New members continue to join in, DDMDPS’s commitment to carry out its mission is ever stronger and greater in scope, in remembrance of Master Sheng Yen’s great teachings. By practicing Chan meditation routinely and implementing the concept of “Spiritual Environmentalism’ at work, many members of DDMDPS find improvement in their interaction with people, leadership, and decision making. Such practices in turn help enhance corporate management, business ethics, employee relations, corporate social responsibility, etc. within their organizations. This paper explores the effects of practicing Chan meditation on body and mind of selected DDMDPS members, as well as the changes they have experienced by applying the concept of “Spiritual Environmentalism’ to work. This paper focuses on the impact of “Spiritual Environmentalism’ on corporate management and corporate social responsibility. Taking DDMDPS members as study samples, this paper attempts to seek evidences to conclude that practicing “Spiritual Environmentalism’ is beneficial to self-growth as well as to corporate management; and that DDMDPS members can set good role models for incorporating “Spiritual Environmentalism’ into company practice to bring greater and better impact on uplifting our society.

Title: Social Engagement of Buddhist Community in Contemporary Taiwan— case study of Juvenile Welfare Institute “Puxian Cihai Juvenile House’
Publisher: Dharma Drum Institute of Liberal Arts
Abstract: “Humanistic Buddhism’ has become the mainstream movement and practical direction for Chinese Buddhism’s developement since 20th Century. Base on the“Humanistic Buddhism’, many of this movement’s practitioners contribute to the Buddhist society greatly in the areas of charity, education and humanity. This research is “Puxian Cihai Juvenile House’, the first buddhist center for misbehavior youth. This research focus on relation between the topics of religion elements’ intervention on juveniles and education on misbehavior youth. This article is a cross domain research include Buddhism and Sociology, mainly with qualitative research with case study method . Data were collected with secondary data and semi-structured interview. This article discuss the practical side of Taiwan’s Buddhism’s involvment with the society, also being out the topic of jail preaching to explain why pick “Puxian Cihai Juvenile House’ for research subject. Besides introduce the case studies, this research also analyze the data to explore whether the founder’s goal implement well within this institution’s operation and management. By analyze this juvenile house’s uniqueness we are to find out if Buddhism’s value bond well to the charity work, also to see if this pioneer work has positive impact and solid contributions to the Buddhism. This research finds that “Puxian Cihai Juvenile House’ is different from other religion involved charity works, the founder is monk who actively involve in prison preaching works, his innovation on establish juvenile house within public religion service center actually deepening the Buddhism’s value to the organization and its participants. Also the organizations’ resource direct benefit the juvenile house. The author believes the necessity of establishment of buddhist juvenile house. It complets the practices of humanistic Buddhism’s hindering view. Base on the mission of “Save one child, the society decrease one possible threat and harm’, “Puxian Cihai Juvenile House’ brings the positive impact to the society and families and holds it speacil role play in the Buddhism’s social involvement.

Title: A study in the Communications of Original Buddhism in Original Buddhism Society
Abstract: Buddhism is one of the most popular religions in Asia. The teaching of Buddhism varied along with the spreading of Buddhism in Asia, even around the world, during the past two thousand and four hundred years, while the Buddhism also faced a great challenge on the adoption to the high development of modern world. In past two hundred years, the research on the pursuit of the original Buddha Śākyamuni’s teaching was conducted in international academia. The practice of Original Buddhism is a new appearance of Humanistic Buddhism. This project is based on research of Original Buddhism Society, their ideas, practices and values for the society. Using the information collected from different media and depth interviews of the key members of Original Buddhism Society, this project is to show how Original Buddhism Society prove original Buddha’s teaching based on the historical and literature analysis, as well as the practice of outcomes in daily life with a basis of the Theory of Relevant Influencing and Four Noble Truths. In addition, the communication strategy of the organization is studied. The research concludes that Original Buddhism Society spreading theory of Relevant Influencing and Four Noble Truths, offer the new opportunity for Buddhism and society to solve the adverse conditions from times and Buddhism itself.

Title: The Ecology of Mind in The Awakening of Faith
